dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond for a sick person at 2100 Main Street, located in front of the bus stop.
Hello sir, my name is Jake and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been sick, nauseated, and I threw up four times. It started about an hour ago.
Right now, what are you experiencing the most pain or discomfort from?
I'm hurting all over my stomach, and my chest feels tight. I also have shortness of breath.
Can you tell me your name and where you are right now?
My name is Marcus, and I'm in front of the bus stop on Main Street.
What day is it today, please?
It's Thursday.
Can you describe what led up to your pain and nausea?
I was trying to walk to the bus stop when my chest started hurting and I got short of breath. I've been feeling this way for about an hour since I started vomiting.
Was there any blood in the vomiting?
No, there was no blood.
Could you tell me about any medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
I have chronic renal failure and need dialysis on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. I also have high blood pressure, diabetes that doesn't need insulin, and some chronic pancreatitis.
And have you ever had any mental health conditions?
Yes, I have major depression.
And are you currently taking any medications?
I take lisinopril, metoprolol, amlodipine-stuff for my blood pressure and kidneys. But today I'm having trouble with my legs-they're really swollen and hard to walk due to the fluid.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how would you rate your chest or stomach pain?
It's a 10, all over my stomach and chest.
Mr. Marcus, we need to do a few things quickly to make sure we can help you. Is that okay?
Sure, go ahead.
I'll check your airway now. Is your throat sore or difficult to breathe through?
No, my throat is fine.
Let's look at your breathing. I don't see a lot of work, you're not tripoding or hypoxic. Can you take a few deep breaths?
Yep, okay.
Respiration rate is 16, and you're not tachypneic.
Oxygen saturation is at 96%; that's within normal limits.
There's no use of accessory muscles. That's a positive sign.
Let me check your wrist pulses. They feel strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 193 over 84. You're not looking flushed or pale.
Your skin is warm and dry. You haven't shown signs of pallor or cyanosis.
You mentioned your legs are stiff and swollen. Could you lift them a little for me?
Yeah, here they are.
Knees and hips look stable. I don't see visible deformities.
Your pulse is 64 and it's regular.
Let's check your blood sugar. The reader is not showing a reading, but no signs of hypoglycemia here.
EKG was performed. Normal sinus rhythm with left atrial enlargement noted, no ST elevation or ectopy observed.
We're going to try and place an IV in one of your arms or legs. Is that okay?
No problem. Do what you have to.
IV placement may help if fluids are needed. It might feel a little pinched, just be ready.
IV placement attempt started now. Image goes in.
As part of our care, we're going to give you some aspirin by mouth. It's 324mg. Will you take it?
Yes, go ahead.
Applied 4-lead ECG monitoring now for continuous surveillance.
12-lead ECG is also recorded. Interpretation shows sinus rhythm with left atrial enlargement, no ST elevation or ectopy noted.
Based on your condition, we're following the medical - abdominal pain protocol. Transport is next.
Where would you like to be transported to?
VCU Health Systems please.
We'll carefully transfer you to the stretcher now. Ready?
Yes, go ahead.
Transferring him to the stretcher now with a pivot and slide method. Secured with straps and rails up.
Second round of vitals shows BP 221/104, heart rate 63, normal rhythm, and oxygen at 99%. SpO₂ is stable.
Any new pain, shortness of breath, or dizziness since we picked you up?
Still feeling sick, but I can talk in full sentences and no new symptoms.
You're alert and oriented-person, place, time, and event. Any changes in your speech or clarity, or are you diaphoretic or pale?
Same: my legs are still stiff and swollen. No changes in vision or hearing. I'm not sweating or pale.
Still a 10 rating pain, and in the same place?
It's a burning sensation in my stomach and chest.
Confirming IV and ECG leads are secure. No glucose reading since device not functioning, but no signs of acute hypoglycemia.
Leads are attached securely and IV is in place. Transport is safe.
We're here to keep you as comfortable as possible. If the pain worsens or you feel worse, let us know immediately, okay?
Got it, thanks.
We may keep the window open and elevate the stretcher. Would you like me to talk to help keep your mind off it?
That would help. Thanks, how did you learn all this?
Through training and real-life experience. As for your condition, you're not showing acute changes or new symptoms.
Symptoms align with known medical history and EKG remains normal. No STE noted.
Correct, no issues so far. I'll keep monitoring.
Responsive, IV in, with stable vitals. GCS is 15, indicating alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
He is not pale, sweating, or in respiratory distress. Speech is clear and full.
VCU, en route with Mr. Marcus, a 52-year-old male, alert and oriented, on IV with a normal GCS in ED room 3.
We appreciate your help throughout, Mr. Marcus. We'll hand you over shortly at the ER. Train is ready.
